1: Psalm 98, like 96, opens with a call to sing a "new song" (šîr ḥādāš) — indicating fresh divine action requiring fresh liturgical response. "For he has done wondrous things (niplāʾôt)" — the past tense grounds the new song in completed divine acts, not aspirations. "His right hand (yĕmînô) and his holy arm (wĕzĕrôaʿ qodšô) have gained him the victory" — the divine warrior's two instruments of battle: the right hand (the weapon-hand, Exod 15:6, 12; Pss 17:7; 18:35; 20:6; 44:3) and the holy arm (the symbol of divine power, Isa 40:10; 51:9; 52:10; 53:1). The "holy arm" of Isa 53:1 ("to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed?") is the arm that brings salvation through the Servant's suffering — the same arm that won the victory in the Exodus now accomplishes the deeper victory of the New Exodus.
2: "The LORD has made his salvation (yĕšûʿāṯô) known and has displayed (gillāh) his righteousness (ṣidqāṯô) before the nations" — divine self-revelation in salvation history. Gālāh (to uncover, to reveal, to make naked) — the divine righteousness uncovered before the nations' eyes, previously hidden. Isaiah 52:10: "the LORD will lay bare his holy arm in the sight of all the nations, and all the ends of the earth will see the salvation of our God." Both texts use the revealing arm of YHWH as the image of divine self-disclosure in salvific action. The "righteousness" shown is not condemnatory judgment but the right-order-restoring salvation — ṣedāqāh as saving righteousness (Isa 45:8; 51:5-6).
3: "He has remembered (zākar) his steadfast love (ḥasdô) and faithfulness (weʾĕmûnāṯô) to the house of Israel. All the ends of the earth have witnessed the salvation of our God" — divine remembering is covenantal action: YHWH "remembers" his covenant by acting on it (Gen 9:15; Exod 2:24; 6:5; Lev 26:42, 45; Ps 105:8; 106:45; 111:5; Jer 31:20; Ezek 16:60). Ḥesed and ʾĕmûnāh — the twin covenant attributes that appear together throughout the Psalter (Ps 36:5; 40:10-11; 57:3; 89:1-2, 14, 24, 33; 92:2; 100:5; 115:1; 117:2; 138:2) and in the divine self-disclosure formula of Exod 34:6-7. "All the ends of the earth" witness the covenant-keeping: what YHWH does for Israel is visible to the whole world and constitutes universal testimony.
4: "Shout for joy (hārîʿû) to the LORD, all the earth! Burst into song and sing praises (pĕṣĕḥû wĕrannĕnû wĕzammērû)!" — three consecutive verbs of exuberant praise: rûaʿ (to shout the war-cry/acclamation), pāṣaḥ (to burst out, to break into song), rānan (to give a ringing cry, to sing joyfully), zāmar (to make music). The accumulation of praise verbs overwhelms the grammatical structure — the praise spills over syntax. The liturgical direction "all the earth" makes the celebration universal; this is not a private Israelite festival but a cosmic event. The enthronement psalms consistently expand beyond the covenant community to include all nations in the proclamation.
5: "Sing praises to the LORD with the harp (kinnôr), with the harp and the sound of singing (qôl zimrāh)!" — the orchestration of worship is specified: the kinnôr (lyre/harp) was the primary stringed instrument of temple worship, associated with David's playing (1 Sam 16:23; 2 Sam 6:5; 1 Chr 25:1-3; Ps 33:2). Qôl zimrāh (voice of melody) — the human voice accompanying the instrument. The combination of instrumental and vocal music represents the full human acoustic offering to God. Hellenistic Judaism and early Christianity continued the harp tradition; Revelation 5:8 describes the twenty-four elders with harps as they sing the new song of the Lamb.
6: "With trumpets (baḥăṣōṣĕrôt) and the blast of the ram's horn (qôl šôpār) shout for joy before the King, the LORD!" — the two primary signaling instruments of Israelite worship are both deployed: ḥăṣōṣĕrāh (silver trumpet, the priestly instrument, Num 10:1-10) and šôpār (ram's horn/shofar, the ancient Israelite alarm and assembly signal). In the Second Temple period, the shofar's blast on Rosh Hashanah (the Jewish New Year) was understood as proclaiming YHWH's kingship at the new year — the liturgical connection between the shofar and divine enthronement remains in the Jewish High Holiday liturgy to this day. "Before the King, the LORD" — the royal title applied directly to YHWH.
7: "Let the sea thunder (yirʿam hayyām) with all that is in it, the world and all who live in it!" — the sea's thunderous voice joins the instrumental ensemble. In Ps 93:3-4, the sea's roaring represents the threatening chaos that YHWH subdues; here the same sea roars in acclamation and worship. The transformation of the sea from chaos-threat to praise-voice is one of the Psalter's great theological moves: the same power that threatened creation now magnifies its creator. "The world (ṯēbēl)" — not just the earth as solid ground but the inhabited world as a totality; the word emphasizes that all the world's inhabitants participate.
8: "Let the rivers clap their hands (nĕhārôt yimḥăʾû-kāp); let the mountains sing together (yĕrannĕnû) for joy!" — the rivers personified with human hands applauding and the mountains with voices singing. The rivers' "clapping hands" is an extraordinary image: the natural phenomenon of water splashing against riverbanks is reimagined as cosmic applause. Isaiah 55:12 provides a close parallel: "the mountains and hills will burst into song before you, and all the trees of the field will clap their hands." The personified creation's praise is the sign of the eschatological restoration — what will happen fully when the King comes to judge anticipates and is rehearsed in the liturgy now.
9: "Before the LORD, for he is coming to judge the earth! He will rule the world with righteousness and the peoples with fairness (mêšārîm)" — the same closing formula as Ps 96:13, with "fairness" (mêšārîm, straightness, equity) replacing "faithfulness" (ʾĕmûnāh) in 96:13. The coming-to-judge is the climax of the entire enthronement celebration — not a threat but the fulfillment of the cosmic order that worship rehearses. The bracketing of the enthronement collection (Pss 96-98) with this identical formula creates a liturgical frame: the new song arises because YHWH is coming; the worship anticipates and proclaims the judgment that will set all things right.
